skill.name: bevy-ecs-expert
skill.description: Master Bevy's Entity Component System (ECS) in Rust, covering Systems, Queries, Resources, and parallel scheduling.
skill.summary: Learn Bevy ECS architecture for high-performance game development in Rust.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis detected patterns from Rust code examples in documentation (commands.spawn() is Bevy ECS entity spawning, not shell commands). This is a pure documentation skill with no executable code. All detections are false positives from markdown code blocks. Safe to publish.

low_findings.title: False Positive: External Commands Pattern
low_findings.description: Static analyzer detected 'external_commands' patterns in Rust code examples. These are Bevy ECS commands.spawn() calls for entity spawning in game code, not shell command execution. The skill contains only documentation with code examples.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Bevy ECS is a Rust game engine; commands.spawn() is the standard API for creating entities in the ECS world, not executing shell commands. The context clearly shows this is documentation for game development.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography Detection
low_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged 'weak cryptographic algorithm' at lines 3 and 17, but these are the YAML frontmatter 'description' field and regular documentation text, not cryptographic code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 3 is YAML frontmatter metadata (description field), line 17 is plain English text about when to use the skill. No cryptographic operations present in the file.

content.user_title: Master Bevy ECS for Game Development
content.value_statement: Build high-performance games with Bevy's data-oriented architecture. This skill teaches you Entity Component System patterns for parallel game logic in Rust.
actual_capabilities: Explain Bevy ECS core concepts (Entities, Components, Systems)
Write efficient Systems using Query types and filters
Design Components and Bundles for game entities
Configure system scheduling and execution order
Optimize game performance with data-oriented patterns
Debug common ECS borrowing and scheduling conflicts
limitations: Requires knowledge of Rust programming fundamentals
Focused on Bevy ECS patterns, not rendering or physics
Does not cover advanced topics like archetypes or internal ECS details

use_cases.title: Learn Bevy from Scratch
use_cases.description: New game developers starting with Bevy can learn ECS concepts, write their first systems, and understand how to structure game logic using data-oriented design.

use_cases.title: Optimize Game Performance
use_cases.description: Experienced developers migrating from object-oriented architectures can refactor their game logic into efficient ECS patterns for better cache utilization and parallel execution.

use_cases.title: Debug System Scheduling
use_cases.description: Developers encountering borrowing conflicts or performance issues can learn proper system ordering, query filters, and resource access patterns to resolve ECS-related errors.

prompt_templates.title: Create Basic Component
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me create a Component for player health in Bevy ECS. Show me the struct definition and explain what derives I need.
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner creating their first Bevy component

prompt_templates.title: Write Movement System
prompt_templates.prompt: I need a system that updates entity positions based on velocity every frame. Write the system function with proper Query types.

prompt_templates.title: Optimize Query Performance
prompt_templates.prompt: My enemy AI system is slow. Help me add query filters like With<Enemy> and Changed<AIState> to only process relevant entities.
prompt_templates.scenario: Optimizing game performance for many entities

prompt_templates.title: Fix Borrowing Conflicts
prompt_templates.prompt: I'm getting a borrow conflict error when two systems access GameState. Show me how to use .chain() or split the resource to fix this.

output_examples.input: Help me create a health component and a system that decreases it over time.
output_examples.output: Here is a Health component and damage system. The component uses #[derive(Component)] to register with Bevy. The system uses Query<&mut Health> to access all entities with health, and Res<Time> for delta time. The system iterates and decreases health by damage_rate * delta_seconds.

output_examples.input: How do I spawn a player entity with multiple components?
output_examples.output: Use a Bundle to combine components. Create a PlayerBundle with Player, Velocity, and SpriteBundle. In your startup system, use commands.spawn(PlayerBundle { ... }) to create the entity atomically. This ensures all components are added together.

output_examples.input: When should I use Res vs ResMut?
output_examples.output: Use Res when you only read the resource, which allows Bevy to run your system in parallel with other read-only systems. Use ResMut when you need to modify the resource, which requires exclusive access and prevents parallel execution with other systems accessing that resource.

best_practices: Use Query filters (With, Without, Changed) to minimize iteration and improve cache efficiency.
Prefer Res over ResMut whenever possible to enable parallel system execution.
Keep Components as pure data without logic; put all behavior in Systems.
Use Bundles to spawn entities with multiple components atomically.
anti_patterns: Avoid storing heavy logic or complex methods inside Components; they should be plain data structs.
Do not use RefCell or interior mutability in Components; let ECS manage borrowing with proper System parameters.
Never mutably access the same component in parallel systems without using .chain() or explicit ordering.

faq.question: What is the difference between Entity and Component?
faq.answer: An Entity is a unique ID, like a container. Components are the data attached to that container. You never store entities directly; you query for the components attached to them.

faq.question: How do I run systems in a specific order?
faq.answer: Use the .chain() method when adding systems to your App, or use SystemSets to define explicit ordering dependencies between groups of systems.

faq.question: Why am I getting a borrow error in my system?
faq.answer: You are trying to access the same data mutably multiple times. Split your query, use different query types, or use .chain() to run systems sequentially instead of in parallel.

faq.question: Can I have multiple of the same component on one entity?
faq.answer: Not directly. Use a Bundle with multiple instances, or store a Vec of your data type within a single component.

faq.question: What is the difference between Query and Res?
faq.answer: Query accesses components attached to entities. Res and ResMut access global Resources, which are singleton data structures like game state, configuration, or asset handles.

faq.question: How do I detect when a component value changes?
faq.answer: Use Query<&YourComponent, Changed<YourComponent>>. This filter ensures your system only processes entities where that component was modified.
